title: 1 Samuel 1
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 There was a man named Elkanah from Ramathaim-zophim in the hills of Ephraim. He was the son of Jeroham, the son of Elihu, the son of Tohu, the son of Zuph, an Ephraimite.
2 He had two wives. One was named Hannah, and the other was Peninnah. Peninnah had children, but Hannah had none.
3 Every year Elkanah went up from his city to Shiloh. He worshiped and made sacrifices to the LORD of Hosts there. Eli's two sons, Hophni and Phinehas, were priests of the LORD there.
4 Whenever the day came for Elkanah to give his sacrifice, he gave portions of meat to his wife Peninnah. He also gave some to all her sons and daughters.
5 But he gave Hannah a double portion, because he loved her. The LORD had kept her from having children.
6 Because the LORD had kept Hannah from having children, the other wife hurt her with cruel words.
7 This went on year after year. Whenever Hannah went up to the house of the LORD, the other wife hurt her. So Hannah cried and would not eat.
8 Her husband Elkanah asked her, "Hannah, why are you crying? Why won't you eat? Why is your heart so sad? Am I not better to you than ten sons?"
9 After they finished eating and drinking in Shiloh, Hannah stood up. Eli the priest was sitting on a chair by the door of the house of the LORD.
10 Hannah was in deep pain. She prayed to the LORD and cried many tears.

11 She made a promise. She said, "O LORD of Hosts, please look at the pain of me, your servant. Please remember me. Do not forget your servant. Give her a son. Then I will give him to the LORD for all his life. No razor will ever touch his head."
12 Hannah kept on praying before the LORD. Eli watched her mouth.
13 Hannah was praying in her heart. Her lips were moving, but her voice could not be heard. So Eli thought she was drunk.
14 Eli said to her, "How long will you be drunk? Put away your wine!"
15 Hannah answered, "No, my lord. I am a woman who is very sad in my heart. I have not had any wine or strong drink. I have told all my heart to the LORD.
16 Do not think I am a bad woman. All this time I have been praying from deep pain and sadness."
17 Eli answered, "Go in peace. May the God of Israel give you the thing you asked Him for."
18 Hannah said, "May your servant be kind to your eyes." Then she went on her way. She began to eat again, and her face was not sad anymore.
19 The next morning Elkanah and Hannah got up early. They bowed in worship before the LORD. Then they returned home to Ramah. Elkanah was with his wife Hannah, and the LORD remembered her.
20 After some time, Hannah became pregnant and gave birth to a son. She named him Samuel. She said, "Because I asked the LORD for him."
21 Then Elkanah and all his family went up to make the yearly sacrifice to the LORD. He also went to keep his promise.
22 But Hannah did not go. She said to her husband, "After the boy is old enough to eat food, I will take him. He will appear before the LORD and stay there forever."
23 Her husband Elkanah replied, "Do what you think is best. Stay here until he is old enough to eat food. May the LORD keep His word." So Hannah stayed and fed her son until he was old enough.

24 When the boy was old enough to eat food, Hannah took him with her. She also took a three-year-old bull, a large bag of flour, and a container of wine. The boy was still young, but she brought him to the house of the LORD at Shiloh.
25 They killed the bull, and then they brought the boy to Eli.
26 Hannah said, "Please, my lord. As you are alive, my lord, I am the woman who stood here beside you. I was praying to the LORD.
27 I prayed for this boy. The LORD has given me what I asked Him for.
28 Now I give the boy to the LORD. For all his life, he belongs to the LORD." Then they worshiped the LORD there.

title: 1 Samuel 2
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 Then Hannah prayed: "My heart is full of joy in the LORD. The LORD lifts my strength high. My mouth speaks with courage against my enemies, for I am glad in your saving help.
2 No one is holy like the LORD. There is no one besides you! And there is no Rock like our God.
3 Do not speak with such pride. Do not let proud words come from your mouth. The LORD is a God who knows. He weighs every action.
4 The bows of the strong are broken, but the weak are filled with strength.
5 Those who had plenty of food now work for bread, but those who were hungry are hungry no more. The woman who had no children now has seven, but the mother of many sons grows weak.
6 The LORD brings death, and he gives life. He brings down to Sheol, and he raises up.
7 The LORD sends poverty, and he sends wealth. He brings low, and he lifts up.

8 He lifts the poor from the dust. He raises the needy from the pile of ashes. He seats them with princes and gives them seats of honor. For the base of the earth belongs to the LORD, and he has set the world on it.
9 He guards the steps of those who trust him, but bad people are lost in darkness. For no one wins by his own strength.

10 Those who fight the LORD will be broken. He will thunder from heaven against them. The LORD will judge the ends of the earth. He will give power to his king. He will lift high the strength of his anointed one."
11 Then Elkanah went home to Ramah. But the boy stayed and served the LORD before Eli the priest.
12 Now the sons of Eli were wicked men. They had no respect for the LORD.
13 They did not follow the right custom for priests with the people. When a man brought a sacrifice, the priest's servant would come with a fork with three points while the meat was boiling.
14 He would push it into the pan, kettle, large pot, or cooking pot. Whatever the fork brought up, the priest took for himself. They treated every Israelite who came to Shiloh this way.

15 Even before they burned the fat, the priest's servant would come. He said to the man who was offering the sacrifice, "Give the priest some meat to roast. He will not take boiled meat from you. He wants only raw meat."
16 If the man said, "The fat must be burned first; then take what you want," the servant would reply, "No, give it to me now. If you refuse, I will take it by force!"
17 So the sin of these young men was very great in the eyes of the LORD. They were treating the LORD's offering with no respect.
18 Now Samuel was serving before the LORD. He was a boy wearing a linen ephod (a priest's vest).
19 Each year his mother made him a little robe. She brought it to him when she went with her husband to offer the yearly sacrifice.
20 Eli would bless Elkanah and his wife. He said, "May the LORD give you children by this woman in place of the boy she gave to the LORD." Then they went home.
21 So the LORD cared for Hannah. She had three more sons and two daughters. Meanwhile, the boy Samuel grew up in the presence of the LORD.
22 Now Eli was very old. He heard about all that his sons were doing to Israel. He heard that they were sleeping with the women who served at the door of the Tent of Meeting.
23 "Why are you doing these things?" Eli said to his sons. "I hear about your wicked deeds from all these people.
24 No, my sons. The report I hear among the LORD's people is not good.

25 If a man sins against another man, God can speak for him. But if a man sins against the LORD, who can speak for him?" But they would not listen to their father, because the LORD had decided to put them to death.
26 And the boy Samuel kept growing. He grew in size and in favor with the LORD and with people.
27 Then a man of God came to Eli. He told him, "This is what the LORD says: 'Did I not clearly show myself to your father's family when they were in Egypt under Pharaoh's house?

28 Out of all the tribes of Israel, I chose your father to be my priest. He was to offer sacrifices on my altar, to burn incense, and to wear an ephod before me. I also gave to your father's family all the fire offerings of the Israelites.

29 Why then do you despise my sacrifice and offering that I commanded for my house? You have honored your sons more than me. You make yourselves fat with the best of every offering from my people Israel.'

30 So the LORD, the God of Israel, says: 'I did say that your family and your father's family would serve before me forever. But now the LORD says: Far from it! I will honor those who honor me. But those who look down on me will be put down.
31 Look, the days are coming when I will take away your strength. I will take the strength of your father's family. No older man will be left in your house.
32 You will see trouble in my house. Even with all the good in Israel, no one in your family will ever grow old again.
33 I will not cut off every one of you from my altar. But the eyes of those left will fail, and their hearts will grieve. All your family after you will die by the sword of men.
34 This will be the sign to you, about your two sons Hophni and Phinehas: They will both die on the same day.
35 Then I will raise up for myself a faithful priest. He will do what is in my heart and mind. I will build for him a lasting family line. He will walk before my anointed one for all time.

36 Everyone left in your family will come and bow down to him. They will ask for a piece of silver or a small piece of bread. They will plead, "Please give me some priestly work so I can eat a piece of bread."'"

title: 1 Samuel 3
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 The boy Samuel served the LORD under Eli. In those days the LORD rarely spoke. Visions were few.
2 Eli's eyes had grown so weak that he could not see. One night he was lying in his room.
3 The lamp of God had not yet gone out. Samuel was lying down in the temple of the LORD. The ark of God was there.
4 Then the LORD called to Samuel. He answered, "Here I am."
5 He ran to Eli and said, "Here I am. You called me." But Eli said, "I did not call you. Go back and lie down." So Samuel went and lay down.
6 Again the LORD called, "Samuel!" Samuel got up and went to Eli. He said, "Here I am. You called me." Eli answered, "My son, I did not call. Go back and lie down."
7 Samuel did not yet know the LORD. The word of the LORD had not yet been shown to him.
8 A third time the LORD called Samuel. He got up, went to Eli, and said, "Here I am. You called me." Then Eli understood that the LORD was calling the boy.
9 So Eli said to Samuel, "Go and lie down. If he calls you, say, 'Speak, LORD, for your servant is listening.'" So Samuel went and lay down in his place.
10 Then the LORD came and stood there. He called as before, "Samuel! Samuel!" Samuel answered, "Speak, for your servant is listening."
11 Then the LORD said to Samuel, "I am about to do something in Israel. The ears of all who hear it will shake.
12 On that day I will do to Eli everything I said about his family. I will do it from beginning to end.
13 I told him I would judge his house forever. He knew his sons' sin. They spoke evil against God, and he did not stop them.
14 So I have sworn to the house of Eli. No sacrifice or offering will ever forgive the sin of his house."
15 Samuel lay down until morning. Then he opened the doors of the house of the LORD. He was afraid to tell Eli the vision.
16 But Eli called to him and said, "Samuel, my son." Samuel answered, "Here I am."
17 Eli asked, "What did the LORD say to you? Do not hide it from me. May God punish you badly if you hide anything he said."
18 So Samuel told him everything. He hid nothing from him. Eli replied, "He is the LORD. Let him do what is good in his eyes."
19 Samuel grew up. The LORD was with him. He made every word of Samuel come true.
20 So all Israel from Dan to Beersheba knew that the LORD had shown Samuel to be a prophet.
21 The LORD kept appearing at Shiloh. There he showed himself to Samuel through his word.

title: 1 Samuel 4
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 So the word of Samuel came to all Israel. The Israelites went out to fight the Philistines. They camped at Ebenezer, and the Philistines camped at Aphek.
2 The Philistines lined up against Israel. As the battle spread, the Philistines defeated Israel. They killed about four thousand men on the battlefield.

3 When the troops came back to camp, the elders of Israel asked, "Why did the LORD let the Philistines defeat us today? Let us bring the ark of the covenant of the LORD from Shiloh. Then it will go with us and save us from our enemies."

4 So the people sent men to Shiloh. They brought back the ark of the covenant of the LORD of Hosts, who sits on his throne between the cherubim. Eli's two sons, Hophni and Phinehas, were there with the ark of the covenant of God.
5 When the ark of the covenant of the LORD came into the camp, all the Israelites shouted loudly. The ground shook from their shout.
6 The Philistines heard the loud shout. They asked, "What is this loud shouting in the camp of the Hebrews?" Then they learned that the ark of the LORD had come into the camp.
7 The Philistines were afraid. They said, "The gods have come into their camp! How sad for us! Nothing like this has happened before.
8 How sad for us! Who will save us from these mighty gods? These are the gods who struck the Egyptians with all kinds of terrible troubles in the wilderness.
9 Be strong and be men, O Philistines! If not, you will serve the Hebrews like they served you. Now be men and fight!"
10 So the Philistines fought, and they defeated Israel. Each man ran away to his tent. The killing was very great — thirty thousand soldiers on foot from Israel fell.
11 The Philistines captured the ark of God. Eli's two sons, Hophni and Phinehas, died.
12 That same day a Benjamite ran from the battle line to Shiloh. His clothes were torn, and dirt was on his head.

13 When he arrived, Eli was sitting on his chair beside the road. He was watching, because his heart was shaking with fear for the ark of God. When the man entered the city to tell the news, the whole city cried out.
14 Eli heard the loud cry. He asked, "Why this loud noise?" So the man hurried over and told Eli.
15 Now Eli was ninety-eight years old. His eyes did not move, because he could not see.
16 The man said to Eli, "I have just come from the battle. I ran from there today." Eli asked, "What happened, my son?"

17 The messenger answered, "Israel has run away from the Philistines. There has been great killing among the people. Your two sons, Hophni and Phinehas, are both dead. The Philistines have captured the ark of God."
18 When the man named the ark of God, Eli fell backward from his chair by the city gate. He was old and heavy, so he broke his neck and died. Eli had judged Israel for forty years.

19 Eli's daughter-in-law, the wife of Phinehas, was about to give birth. She heard the news: God's ark was captured, and her father-in-law and husband were dead. She fell down and gave birth, for the birth pains came over her.
20 As she was dying, the women caring for her said, "Do not be afraid. You have given birth to a son!" But she did not answer or listen.
21 She named the boy **Ichabod — the glory has departed from Israel**. She said this because the ark of God was captured, and her father-in-law and her husband were dead.
22 She said, "**The glory has departed from Israel**, for the ark of God has been captured."

title: 1 Samuel 5
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 The Philistines captured the ark of God. They took it from Ebenezer to Ashdod.
2 They carried it into the temple of Dagon. They put it next to his statue.
3 The people of Ashdod got up early the next morning. There lay Dagon, face down before the ark of the LORD. So they lifted Dagon and put him back in his place.
4 They got up early again the next morning. There lay Dagon, face down before the ark of the LORD. His head and his hands were cut off. They lay on the doorway. Only his body was left.
5 That is why the priests of Dagon do not step on the doorway. All who enter the temple of Dagon in Ashdod also avoid it, to this day.
6 Then the LORD's hand pressed hard on the people of Ashdod. He hurt them and the nearby towns. He made them sick with painful growths.
7 The men of Ashdod saw what was happening. They said, "The ark of the God of Israel must not stay here with us. His hand is heavy on us and on our god Dagon."

8 So they called all the leaders of the Philistines together. They asked, "What shall we do with the ark of the God of Israel?" The leaders replied, "Move it to Gath." So they carried the ark of the God of Israel there.
9 But after they moved the ark to Gath, the LORD's hand turned against that city too. He filled the city with great fear and trouble. He made the men sick with painful growths, both young and old.
10 So they sent the ark of God to Ekron. But as it arrived, the Ekronites cried out. They said, "They have brought us the ark of the God of Israel to kill us and our people!"

11 Then the Ekronites called all the leaders of the Philistines together. They said, "Send the ark of the God of Israel away. It must return to its place, so it will not kill us and our people!" A deadly fear had spread through the city. The hand of God was heavy on it.
12 Those who did not die became sick with painful growths. The cries of the city went up to heaven.

title: 1 Samuel 6
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 The ark of the LORD stayed in the land of the Philistines for seven months.
2 The Philistines called their priests and fortune-tellers. They asked, "What shall we do with the ark of the LORD? Tell us how to send it back."
3 They answered, "If you send back the ark of the God of Israel, do not send it empty. Send it with a gift to say sorry. Then you will be healed. Then you will know why He keeps hurting you."
4 The Philistines asked, "What gift should we send back?" They said, "Five gold sores and five gold rats. That matches the number of Philistine rulers. The same trouble has hit you and your rulers."
5 "Make models of your sores and of the rats that are ruining the land. Give glory to the God of Israel. Then maybe He will stop hurting you, your gods, and your land.
6 Why make your hearts stubborn, as the Egyptians and Pharaoh did? When God hurt them, they finally let the people go.
7 Now make one new cart. Take two milk cows that have never been tied to a yoke. Tie the cows to the cart. Take their calves away and shut them in.
8 Put the ark of the LORD on the cart. Put the gold objects in a box beside it as your gift to say sorry. Then send the ark on its way.
9 But watch it carefully. If it goes up the road toward Beth-shemesh, then the LORD brought this trouble on us. If not, we will know His hand did not punish us. It just happened by chance."
10 The men did what they were told. They took two milk cows and tied them to the cart. They shut the calves in.
11 Then they put the ark of the LORD on the cart. They also put the box with the gold rats and the models of the sores.

12 The cows went straight up the road toward Beth-shemesh. They stayed on that one road and kept mooing. They did not turn right or left. The Philistine rulers followed them to the border of Beth-shemesh.
13 The people of Beth-shemesh were cutting wheat in the valley. When they looked up and saw the ark, they were very happy.
14 The cart came to the field of Joshua of Beth-shemesh and stopped near a large rock. The people chopped up the cart. They offered the cows as a burnt offering to the LORD.

15 The Levites took down the ark of the LORD and the box with the gold objects. They placed them on the large rock. That day the men of Beth-shemesh offered burnt offerings and sacrifices to the LORD.
16 When the five Philistine rulers saw this, they went back to Ekron that same day.
17 The Philistines sent back one gold sore for each city as a gift to say sorry to the LORD. The cities were Ashdod, Gaza, Ashkelon, Gath, and Ekron.

18 The gold rats also matched the number of Philistine cities. These belonged to the five rulers — the strong-walled cities and the small villages near them. The large rock where they placed the ark still stands today in the field of Joshua of Beth-shemesh.
19 But God killed some men of Beth-shemesh because they looked inside the ark of the LORD. He killed seventy men. The people mourned because the LORD had struck them with a great killing.
20 The men of Beth-shemesh asked, "Who can stand before the LORD, this holy God? Where should the ark go from here?"
21 So they sent messengers to the people of Kiriath-jearim. They said, "The Philistines have sent back the ark of the LORD. Come down and take it up with you."

title: 1 Samuel 7
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 Then the men of Kiriath-jearim came for the ark of the LORD. They took it to Abinadab's house on the hill. They set apart his son Eleazar to guard the ark.
2 A long time passed — twenty years in all. The ark stayed at Kiriath-jearim. All the people of Israel were sad and looked for the LORD.

3 Then Samuel said to all Israel, "If you are coming back to the LORD with all your hearts, get rid of the foreign gods and the Ashtoreths among you. Make your hearts ready for the LORD. Serve only Him. Then He will save you from the power of the Philistines."
4 So the Israelites put away the Baals and Ashtoreths. They served only the LORD.
5 Then Samuel said, "Gather all Israel to Mizpah. I will pray to the LORD for you."

6 When they had gathered at Mizpah, they got water and poured it out before the LORD. That day they did not eat. There they said openly, "We have sinned against the LORD." And Samuel led the Israelites at Mizpah.
7 The Philistines heard that the Israelites had gathered at Mizpah. So their rulers marched up against Israel. When the Israelites learned of this, they were afraid of the Philistines.
8 They said to Samuel, "Do not stop calling out to the LORD our God for us. Then He may save us from the power of the Philistines."
9 Then Samuel took a young lamb still drinking milk. He offered it as a whole burnt offering to the LORD. He called out to the LORD for Israel, and the LORD answered him.

10 The Philistines came near to fight Israel as Samuel was offering the sacrifice. But that day the LORD sent loud thunder against the Philistines. He made them so confused that they ran away from Israel.
11 Then the men of Israel rushed out of Mizpah and chased the Philistines. They beat them all the way to a place below Beth-car.
12 After this, Samuel took a stone and set it up between Mizpah and Shen. He named it **Ebenezer — Thus far the LORD has helped us**.
13 So the Philistines were beaten. They stopped attacking the land of Israel. The hand of the LORD was against the Philistines all the days of Samuel.

14 The cities from Ekron to Gath, which the Philistines had taken, were given back to Israel. Israel also freed the land around them from the Philistines. And there was peace between the Israelites and the Amorites.
15 So Samuel led Israel all the days of his life.
16 Every year he made a regular trip from Bethel to Gilgal to Mizpah. He led Israel in all these places.
17 Then he would return to Ramah, because his home was there. There he led Israel and built an altar to the LORD.

title: 1 Samuel 8
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 When Samuel grew old, he made his sons judges over Israel.
2 His firstborn son was Joel. His second son was Abijah. They were judges in Beersheba.
3 But his sons did not live like him. They wanted money in wrong ways. They took bribes and twisted justice.
4 So all the leaders of Israel came together to Samuel at Ramah.
5 They said, "Look, you are old. Your sons do not live like you. **Give us a king to lead us, such as all the other nations have**."
6 But this upset Samuel when they said, "Give us a king to judge us." So Samuel prayed to the LORD.
7 The LORD said to Samuel, "Listen to all the people say to you. **It is not you they have rejected, but they have rejected me as their king**.
8 They have done this from the day I brought them out of Egypt. They keep leaving me and serving other gods. Now they do the same to you.
9 Listen to them, but warn them clearly. Show them what the king will do when he rules over them."
10 So Samuel told all the words of the LORD to the people who asked for a king.
11 He said, "This is what the king will do when he rules you. He will take your sons for his chariots and horses. They will run in front of his chariots.
12 He will make some leaders over thousands and over fifties. Others will plow his fields and gather his crops. Others will make his weapons and equip his chariots.
13 He will take your daughters to make perfume, cook, and bake for him.
14 He will take your best fields, vineyards, and olive fields. He will give them to his servants.
15 He will take one tenth of your grain and grapes. He will give it to his officials and servants.
16 He will take your male and female servants. He will take your best cattle and donkeys for his own use.
17 He will take one tenth of your flocks. And you yourselves will become his slaves.
18 On that day, you will cry out for help from the king you chose. But the LORD will not answer you on that day."
19 But the people refused to listen to Samuel. They said, "No! We must have a king over us.
20 Then we will be like all the other nations. Our king will judge us, lead us, and fight our battles."
21 Samuel listened to all the words of the people. Then he told them to the LORD.
22 The LORD said to Samuel, "Listen to their voice. Give them a king." Then Samuel said to the men of Israel, "Everyone, go back to your city."

title: 1 Samuel 9
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 There was a man from the tribe of Benjamin. He was strong and important. His name was Kish. He was the son of Abiel, son of Zeror, son of Becorath, son of Aphiah of Benjamin.
2 Kish had a son named Saul. Saul was a fine young man, good-looking. No one in Israel looked better than him. He was taller than everyone by a head.
3 One day the donkeys of Kish, Saul's father, got lost. So Kish said to his son Saul, "Take one of the servants and go look for the donkeys."

4 Saul went through the hills of Ephraim. Then he went through the land of Shalishah. But they did not find the donkeys. They went through the area of Shaalim. The donkeys were not there. Then they went through the land of Benjamin. Still they did not find them.
5 When they reached the land of Zuph, Saul said to his servant, "Come, let us go back. My father will stop worrying about the donkeys and start worrying about us."
6 "Look," said the servant, "there is a man of God in this city. He is well respected. Everything he says comes true. Let us go there now. Maybe he will tell us which way to go."
7 "If we go," Saul replied, "what can we give the man? The bread in our bags is gone. We have no gift to take to the man of God. What do we have?"
8 The servant answered him again. "Look," he said, "I have a small piece of silver in my hand. I will give it to the man of God. Then he will tell us our way."
9 (Long ago in Israel, when a man went to ask God, he would say, "Come, let us go to the seer." The prophet of today was once called the seer.)
10 "Good," said Saul to his servant. "Come, let us go." So they set out for the city where the man of God was.
11 As they were going up the hill to the city, they met some young women. The women were coming out to draw water. Saul asked them, "Is the seer here?"
12 "Yes, he is ahead of you," they answered. "Hurry now. Today he has come to the city. The people have a sacrifice on the high place.

13 As soon as you enter the city, you will find him. He has not yet gone up to the high place to eat. The people will not eat until he comes. He must pray over the sacrifice first. After that, the guests will eat. Go up at once. You will find him."
14 So Saul and his servant went up to the city. As they were entering, Samuel was coming toward them. He was on his way up to the high place.
15 The day before Saul came, the LORD had told Samuel,

16 "At this time tomorrow I will send you a man. He will come from the land of Benjamin. You must pour oil on him as leader over My people Israel. He will save them from the hand of the Philistines. I have looked on My people, because I have heard their cry."
17 When Samuel saw Saul, the LORD told him, "Here is the man I spoke about. He will rule over My people."
18 Saul came near to Samuel in the gateway. He asked, "Please tell me where the seer's house is."
19 "I am the seer," Samuel replied. "Go up before me to the high place. You shall eat with me today. In the morning I will send you off. I will tell you all that is in your heart.
20 As for the donkeys you lost three days ago, do not worry. They have been found. And on whom is all the desire of Israel? Is it not on you and all your father's family?"
21 Saul replied, "Am I not from Benjamin, the smallest tribe of Israel? Is not my family the smallest of all the families in Benjamin? Why do you say such a thing to me?"
22 Then Samuel took Saul and his servant. He brought them into the hall. He gave them the best seats among the guests. About thirty people were invited.
23 Samuel said to the cook, "Bring the portion I gave you. I told you to keep it back."

24 So the cook picked up the leg and what was on it. He set it before Saul. Then Samuel said, "Here is what was kept back. It was saved for you. Eat it. It has been kept for you for this time, from when I said, 'I have invited the people.'" So Saul ate with Samuel that day.
25 After they came down from the high place into the city, Samuel spoke with Saul on the roof of his house.
26 They got up early in the morning. Just before dawn, Samuel called to Saul on the roof. "Get ready, and I will send you on your way!" So Saul got up. He and Samuel went outside together.

27 As they were going down to the edge of the city, Samuel said to Saul, "Tell the servant to go on ahead of us. But you stay here for a while. I will tell you a message from God." So the servant went on.

title: 1 Samuel 10
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 Then Samuel took a small jar of oil and poured it on Saul's head. He kissed him and said, "The LORD has chosen you as leader over His own people.

2 When you leave me today, you will meet two men at Rachel's tomb. It is at Zelzah, on the border of Benjamin. They will say to you, 'The donkeys you went to find have been found. Now your father is not worried about the donkeys. He is worried about you. He keeps asking, "What should I do about my son?"'

3 From there you will go on to the Oak of Tabor. Three men will meet you there. They are going up to God at Bethel. One will carry three young goats. Another will carry three loaves of bread. Another will carry a bag of wine.
4 They will greet you and give you two loaves of bread. You will take the bread from their hands.

5 After that you will come to Gibeah of God. The Philistines have an army camp there. As you reach the city, you will meet a group of prophets. They will come down from the high place. They will have harps, tambourines, flutes, and lyres in front of them. They will be speaking God's message.
6 Then the Spirit of the LORD will come on you with power. You will speak God's message with them. You will be changed into a different person.
7 When these signs happen, do what the moment needs. For God is with you.
8 Go down to Gilgal before me. I will surely come to you to offer burnt offerings and peace offerings. Wait seven days until I come and tell you what to do."
9 As Saul turned to leave Samuel, God changed Saul's heart. All the signs came true that day.
10 When Saul and his servant came to Gibeah, a group of prophets met him. Then the Spirit of God came on him with power. He spoke God's message with them.
11 All those who had known Saul before saw him speaking God's message with the prophets. They asked one another, "What has happened to the son of Kish? Is Saul also among the prophets?"
12 Then a man who lived there replied, "And who is their father?" So the saying became a common saying: "Is Saul also among the prophets?"
13 When Saul had finished speaking God's message, he went up to the high place.
14 Now Saul's uncle asked him and his servant, "Where did you go?" Saul replied, "To look for the donkeys. When we could not find them, we went to Samuel."
15 Saul's uncle asked, "Tell me, what did Samuel say to you?"
16 Saul replied, "He told us that the donkeys had been found." But Saul did not tell his uncle what Samuel had said about being king.
17 After this, Samuel called the people together to the LORD at Mizpah.

18 He said to the Israelites, "This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: 'I brought Israel up out of Egypt. I rescued you from the hand of the Egyptians. I rescued you from all the kingdoms that held you down.'
19 But today you have rejected your God. He saves you from all your troubles and pain. You have said to Him, 'No, set a king over us.' Now come before the LORD by your tribes and family groups."
20 So Samuel had all the tribes of Israel come forward. The tribe of Benjamin was chosen.
21 Then he had the tribe of Benjamin come forward by its family groups. The clan of Matri was chosen. Finally, Saul son of Kish was chosen. But when they looked for him, they could not find him.
22 So they asked the LORD again, "Has the man come here yet?" The LORD replied, "Look, he has hidden himself among the bags."
23 So they ran and brought Saul. When he stood among the people, he was taller than any of them by a head.
24 Samuel said to all the people, "Do you see the one the LORD has chosen? There is no one like him among all the people." And all the people shouted, "Long live the king!"
25 Then Samuel explained to the people the rules for being king. He wrote them on a scroll and placed it before the LORD. Then Samuel sent all the people away, each to his own home.
26 Saul also went to his home in Gibeah. The brave men whose hearts God had touched went with him.
27 But some bad men said, "How can this man save us?" So they hated him and brought him no gifts. But Saul stayed silent about it.

title: 1 Samuel 11
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 Soon Nahash the Ammonite came and attacked Jabesh-gilead. All the men of Jabesh said to him, "Make an agreement with us, and we will serve you."
2 But Nahash the Ammonite answered, "I will make an agreement with you on one condition. I will take out the right eye of every man and bring shame on all Israel."
3 The elders of Jabesh said, "Wait seven days. Let us send messengers through all Israel. If no one saves us, we will give ourselves up to you."
4 The messengers came to Gibeah of Saul and told these words to the people. Then all the people cried out loud.
5 Just then Saul was coming back from the field, behind his oxen. "What is wrong with the people?" Saul asked. "Why are they crying?" They told him the words of the men from Jabesh.
6 When Saul heard their words, the Spirit of God came on him with power. He became very angry.

7 He took two oxen and cut them into pieces. He sent the pieces by messengers through all the land of Israel. He said, "This is what will happen to the oxen of any man who does not follow Saul and Samuel." Then fear from the LORD fell on the people, and they came out together as one.
8 When Saul counted them at Bezek, there were 300,000 men of Israel and 30,000 men of Judah.
9 So they said to the messengers, "Tell the men of Jabesh-gilead: 'We will save you tomorrow before the sun gets hot.'" When the messengers told this to the men of Jabesh, they were happy.
10 Then the men of Jabesh said to Nahash, "Tomorrow we will come out to you. You can do with us whatever seems good to you."

11 The next day Saul divided his soldiers into three groups. Early in the morning they attacked the camp of the Ammonites and killed them. They fought until the hottest part of the day. The men left alive were spread so far apart that no two stayed together.
12 Then the people said to Samuel, "Who said that Saul should not rule over us? Bring those men here so we can kill them!"
13 But Saul said, "No one will be put to death today. Today the LORD has saved Israel."
14 Then Samuel said to the people, "Come, let us go to Gilgal and make Saul king again there."
15 So all the people went to Gilgal. There they made Saul king before the LORD. They offered peace offerings before the LORD. And Saul and all the people of Israel were very happy.

title: 1 Samuel 12
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 Then Samuel said to all Israel, "I have heard your voice in everything. I have set a king over you.
2 Now the king walks before you. I am old, and my hair is gray. My sons are here with you. I have walked before you from my youth until today.

3 Here I am. Speak against me before the LORD and His chosen one. Whose ox or donkey have I taken? Whom have I hurt or treated badly? From whose hand have I taken secret money and closed my eyes? Tell me, and I will give it back."
4 They replied, "You have not hurt us or treated us badly. You have not taken anything from any man's hand."
5 Samuel said to them, "The LORD is a witness against you. His chosen one is a witness today. You have found nothing in my hand." They replied, "He is a witness."
6 Then Samuel said to the people, "The LORD chose Moses and Aaron. He brought your fathers out of the land of Egypt.
7 Now stand here. Before the LORD, I will tell you all His good works. He did them for you and your fathers.
8 When Jacob went to Egypt, your fathers called out to the LORD. He sent them Moses and Aaron. They brought your fathers out of Egypt and settled them here.
9 But they forgot the LORD their God. So He sold them into the hand of Sisera, the army leader of Hazor. He also sold them to the Philistines and the king of Moab. These enemies fought against them.
10 Then they called out to the LORD. They said, 'We have sinned. We left the LORD and served the Baals and Ashtoreths. Now save us from our enemies, and we will serve You.'
11 So the LORD sent Jerubbaal, Barak, Jephthah, and Samuel. He saved you from your enemies all around. So you lived safely.
12 But you saw that Nahash, king of the Ammonites, was coming against you. You said to me, 'No, we must have a king to rule us.' But the LORD your God was already your king.
13 Now here is the king you chose. You asked for him. Look — the LORD has placed a king over you.
14 Fear the LORD. Serve Him. Obey His voice. Do not turn against His command. You and the king must follow the LORD your God. Then all will be well.
15 But if you disobey and turn against His command, His hand will be against you. It was the same against your fathers.
16 Now stand and see this great thing. The LORD will do it before your eyes.
17 Is it not the wheat harvest today? I will call on the LORD to send thunder and rain. Then you will know and see the great evil you did. You asked for a king before the LORD."
18 So Samuel called to the LORD. On that day the LORD sent thunder and rain. So all the people greatly feared the LORD and Samuel.
19 They begged Samuel, "Pray to the LORD your God for your servants. We do not want to die. We added to our sins the evil of asking for a king."
20 Samuel replied, "Do not be afraid. You have done all this evil. But do not turn away from the LORD. Serve the LORD with all your heart.
21 Do not turn away after useless things. They cannot help you or save you. They are empty.
22 For the sake of His great name, the LORD will not leave His people. He was pleased to make you His own.
23 As for me, I will never stop praying for you. That would be a sin against the LORD. I will keep teaching you the good and right way.
24 Above all, fear the LORD and serve Him faithfully with all your heart. Think about the great things He has done for you.
25 But if you keep on doing evil, both you and your king will be destroyed."

title: 1 Samuel 13
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 Saul was thirty years old when he became king. He ruled over Israel for forty-two years.

2 Saul chose three thousand men of Israel for himself. Two thousand were with Saul at Michmash and the hills of Bethel. A thousand were with Jonathan in Gibeah of Benjamin. He sent the rest of the men home.
3 Then Jonathan attacked the Philistine army post at Geba. The Philistines heard about it. So Saul blew the trumpet across the land. He said, "Let the Hebrews hear!"
4 All Israel heard the news. "Saul has attacked a Philistine post. Now Israel has become hated by the Philistines!" Then the people were called to join Saul at Gilgal.

5 The Philistines came together to fight Israel. They had three thousand war chariots and six thousand men on horses. Their soldiers were as many as sand on the seashore. They went up and camped at Michmash, east of Beth-aven.
6 The men of Israel saw that they were in great trouble. They hid in caves and bushes, among the rocks, and in holes and water pits.
7 Some Hebrews even crossed the Jordan into Gad and Gilead. But Saul stayed at Gilgal. All his men were shaking with fear.
8 Saul waited seven days for the time Samuel had set. But Samuel did not come to Gilgal. Saul's men began to leave him.
9 So Saul said, "Bring me the burnt offering and the peace offerings." Then he offered up the burnt offering.
10 Just as he finished the burnt offering, Samuel arrived. Saul went out to greet him.
11 "What have you done?" Samuel asked. Saul replied, "I saw that my men were leaving me. You did not come at the set time. The Philistines were gathering at Michmash.
12 I thought, 'Now the Philistines will come down on me at Gilgal. And I have not asked the LORD for help.' So I felt I had to offer the burnt offering."

13 "You have done a foolish thing," Samuel said. "You have not kept the command that the LORD your God gave you. If you had kept it, the LORD would have made your kingdom strong over Israel for all time.
14 But now your kingdom will not last. **The LORD has sought out a man after his own heart**. He has chosen him as ruler over his people. This is because you have not kept the LORD's command."
15 Then Samuel left Gilgal and went up to Gibeah in Benjamin. Saul counted the men with him — about six hundred.
16 Saul and his son Jonathan and their men were staying in Geba of Benjamin. The Philistines were camped at Michmash.
17 Groups of attackers went out of the Philistine camp in three parts. One group went toward Ophrah in the land of Shual.
18 Another group went toward Beth-horon. The third group went down the border road. That road looks over the Valley of Zeboim toward the wilderness.
19 No metal worker could be found in all Israel. The Philistines had said, "The Hebrews must not make swords or spears."
20 So all the Israelites went down to the Philistines. They went to sharpen their farm tools.
21 The charge was a small payment to sharpen a plow blade or hoe. It cost a third of a shekel to sharpen a fork or axe. It cost the same to sharpen a long stick used for oxen.
22 So on the day of battle, no sword or spear was in the hands of Saul's men or Jonathan's men. Only Saul and his son Jonathan had weapons.
23 A group of Philistine soldiers had gone out to the pass at Michmash.

title: 1 Samuel 14
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 One day Jonathan, son of Saul, spoke to his young armor-bearer. He said, "Come, let us cross over to the Philistine camp." But Jonathan did not tell his father.
2 Saul was staying under the pomegranate tree in Migron, near Gibeah. About six hundred men were with him.

3 Ahijah the priest was also there, wearing an ephod. He was the son of Ahitub, brother of Ichabod. Ahitub was the son of Phinehas, son of Eli, the LORD's priest in Shiloh. The troops did not know Jonathan had left.
4 There were cliffs on both sides of the pass Jonathan planned to cross. One was named Bozez, and the other was named Seneh.
5 One cliff stood to the north, toward Michmash. The other stood to the south, toward Geba.
6 Jonathan said to his armor-bearer, "Come, let us cross over to these foreign men. Maybe the LORD will act for us. Nothing can stop the LORD from saving, by many or by few."
7 His armor-bearer replied, "Do all that is in your heart. Go ahead; I am with you, heart and soul."
8 "Very well," said Jonathan. "We will cross over and show ourselves to these men.
9 If they say, 'Wait until we come to you,' we will stay here. We will not go up to them.
10 But if they say, 'Come on up,' then we will go up. That will be our sign that the LORD has given them to us."
11 So the two men showed themselves to the Philistine camp. The Philistines said, "Look, the Hebrews are coming out of their hiding holes!"
12 The men of the camp called to Jonathan and his armor-bearer. "Come on up, and we will teach you a lesson!" Jonathan said to his armor-bearer, "Follow me. The LORD has given them to Israel."
13 So Jonathan climbed up on his hands and feet. His armor-bearer climbed up behind him. The Philistines fell before Jonathan, and his armor-bearer finished them off.
14 In that first attack, Jonathan and his armor-bearer killed about twenty men. They did this over half an acre of land.
15 Great fear came on the Philistines in the camp and in the field. The men in the outposts and raiding groups also shook with fear. The earth shook, and panic came from God.
16 Saul's watchmen at Gibeah in Benjamin saw the Philistine troops melting away. The troops were running in every direction.
17 Saul said to his troops, "Call the roll. See who has left us." When they called the roll, Jonathan and his armor-bearer were missing.
18 Then Saul said to Ahijah, "Bring the ark of God." At that time it was with the Israelites.
19 While Saul was talking to the priest, the noise in the Philistine camp grew louder. So Saul said to the priest, "Stop. Pull your hand back."
20 Then Saul and all his troops gathered and went to the battle. They found the Philistines in total confusion, each man fighting his neighbor with the sword.
21 Some Hebrews had earlier joined the Philistine camps. Now they came over to the Israelites with Saul and Jonathan.
22 All the Israelites hiding in the hill country of Ephraim heard the Philistines were running away. They also joined Saul and Jonathan in the battle.
23 So the LORD saved Israel that day. The battle moved on past Beth-aven.

24 The men of Israel were in great trouble that day. Saul had put the troops under an oath. He had said, "A curse will fall on any man who eats food before evening. I must first take revenge on my enemies." So no one tasted any food.
25 Then all the troops entered the forest. There was honey on the ground.
26 When they saw the flowing honey, no one put his hand to his mouth. They were afraid of the oath.
27 But Jonathan had not heard his father's oath. So he reached out the staff in his hand. He dipped its end into the honeycomb and ate. His eyes grew bright.
28 Then a soldier told him, "Your father bound the troops with a strong oath. He said, 'A curse will fall on any man who eats food today.' That is why the people are weak."
29 Jonathan replied, "My father has brought trouble to the land. Look how my eyes grew bright after I tasted a little honey.
30 It would have been much better if the troops had eaten today. They could have taken food from the goods of their enemies. Would not the defeat of the Philistines have been even greater?"
31 That day the Israelites struck down the Philistines from Michmash to Aijalon. After that, the people were very weak.
32 So they rushed to the goods they had taken. They grabbed sheep, cattle, and calves. They killed them on the ground and ate the meat with the blood still in it.
33 Then someone told Saul, "Look, the troops are sinning against the LORD. They are eating meat with the blood still in it." Saul said, "You have done wrong. Roll a large stone over here at once."

34 Then Saul said, "Go to the troops. Tell each man to bring me his ox or sheep. Let him kill it here and then eat. Do not sin against the LORD by eating meat with the blood." So that night each man brought his ox and killed it there.
35 Then Saul built an altar to the LORD. It was the first altar he had built to the LORD.

36 Saul said, "Let us chase the Philistines by night. We will take their goods until dawn and leave no man alive." The troops replied, "Do what seems good to you." But the priest said, "We must ask God first."
37 So Saul asked God, "Shall I chase the Philistines? Will You give them to Israel?" But God did not answer him that day.
38 Then Saul said, "Come here, all you leaders of the troops. Let us find out how this sin happened today.
39 As surely as the LORD who saves Israel lives, he must die. Even if it is my son Jonathan, he must die." But no one in the troops said a word.
40 Then Saul said to all Israel, "You stand on one side. My son Jonathan and I will stand on the other side." The troops said, "Do what seems good to you."

41 So Saul prayed to the LORD, the God of Israel. "Why have You not answered Your servant today? If the fault is with me or Jonathan, answer with Urim. If the fault is with Israel, answer with Thummim." Jonathan and Saul were chosen, but the people were cleared.
42 Then Saul said, "Cast the lot between me and my son Jonathan." And Jonathan was chosen.
43 Saul said to him, "Tell me what you have done." Jonathan answered, "I only tasted a little honey with the end of my staff. And now I must die?"
44 Saul said, "May God punish me, and even worse, if you do not die, Jonathan!"

45 But the people said to Saul, "Must Jonathan die? He brought this great rescue to Israel! Never! As surely as the LORD lives, not a hair of his head will fall to the ground. He did this today with God's help." So the people rescued Jonathan, and he did not die.
46 Then Saul stopped chasing the Philistines. The Philistines returned to their own land.

47 After Saul became king over Israel, he fought all his enemies on every side. He fought the Moabites, the Ammonites, the Edomites, the kings of Zobah, and the Philistines. Wherever he turned, he defeated them.
48 He fought bravely and defeated the Amalekites. He rescued Israel from those who took their goods.
49 The sons of Saul were Jonathan, Ishvi, and Malchishua. His two daughters were Merab, the older one, and Michal, the younger one.
50 His wife's name was Ahinoam, daughter of Ahimaaz. The leader of his army was Abner, son of Saul's uncle Ner.
51 Saul's father Kish and Abner's father Ner were both sons of Abiel.
52 The war with the Philistines was fierce all the days of Saul. So whenever Saul saw a strong or brave man, he took him into his army.

title: 1 Samuel 15
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 Then Samuel said to Saul, "The LORD sent me to choose you as king over His people Israel.
2 Now listen to the words of the LORD. The LORD of Hosts says this: 'I saw what the Amalekites did to the Israelites. They attacked them on the road from Egypt.
3 Now go and attack the Amalekites. Destroy completely all that belongs to them. Do not spare them. Kill men and women, children and babies, oxen and sheep, camels and donkeys.'"
4 So Saul called the soldiers together. He counted them at Telaim — 200,000 foot soldiers, and 10,000 men from Judah.
5 Saul came to the city of Amalek. He waited in secret in the valley.

6 He warned the Kenites, "You were kind to the Israelites when they came out of Egypt. So move away from the Amalekites. If you stay, I will destroy you too." So the Kenites moved away from the Amalekites.
7 Then Saul defeated the Amalekites. He fought them from Havilah all the way to Shur, east of Egypt.
8 He caught Agag, the king of Amalek, alive. But he destroyed all the others with the sword.

9 Saul and his soldiers spared Agag. They also kept the best sheep and cattle, the fat calves and lambs, and the best of everything. They did not want to destroy these. But they destroyed everything that was poor and weak.
10 Then the word of the LORD came to Samuel. He said,
11 "I am sorry that I made Saul king. He has stopped following Me and has not done what I told him." Samuel was very upset. He cried out to the LORD all that night.
12 Early in the morning Samuel got up to meet Saul. But someone told him, "Saul has gone to Carmel. He has built a stone marker for himself. Then he went down to Gilgal."
13 When Samuel reached him, Saul said, "May the LORD bless you. I have done what the LORD told me."
14 But Samuel replied, "Then what is this sound of sheep and cattle that I hear?"
15 Saul answered, "The soldiers brought them from the Amalekites. They kept the best sheep and cattle to sacrifice to the LORD your God. But we destroyed the rest."
16 "Stop!" Samuel said. "Let me tell you what the LORD said to me last night." "Tell me," Saul replied.
17 Samuel said, "Once you were small in your own eyes. But now you are the leader of the tribes of Israel. The LORD chose you as king over Israel.
18 The LORD sent you on a mission. He said, 'Go and destroy the sinful Amalekites completely. Fight them until they are all gone.'
19 So why did you not obey the LORD? Why did you rush to take the goods? Why did you do evil in the sight of the LORD?"
20 "But I did obey the LORD," Saul replied. "I went on the mission the LORD gave me. I brought back Agag, the king of Amalek. I destroyed the Amalekites.
21 The soldiers took sheep and cattle from the goods. They took the best things to sacrifice to the LORD your God at Gilgal."
22 But Samuel said, "Does the LORD delight in burnt offerings and sacrifices as much as in obeying His voice? Look — **To obey is better than sacrifice, and to heed is better than the fat of rams**.
23 Refusing to obey is like magic, a sin. Pride is like worshiping idols, a great evil. You refused the word of the LORD. So He has refused you as king."
24 Then Saul said to Samuel, "I have sinned. I broke the LORD's command and your instructions. I was afraid of the people and obeyed their voice.
25 Now please forgive my sin. Come back with me so I can worship the LORD."
26 Samuel replied, "I will not return with you. You refused the word of the LORD. So He has refused you as king over Israel."
27 As Samuel turned to go, Saul grabbed the edge of his coat. The coat tore.
28 So Samuel said to him, "**The LORD has torn the kingdom of Israel from you today and has given it to one of your neighbors — to one better than you**.
29 The Glory of Israel does not lie or change His mind. He is not a man, that He should change His mind."
30 "I have sinned," Saul replied. "Please show me honor now in front of the leaders of my people and in front of Israel. Come back with me, so I may worship the LORD your God."
31 So Samuel went back with Saul, and Saul worshiped the LORD.
32 Then Samuel said, "Bring me Agag, the king of the Amalekites." Agag came to him with a happy face. He thought, "Surely the bitter pain of death is past."
33 But Samuel said, "Your sword has killed the sons of many mothers. So your own mother will lose her son too." Then Samuel cut Agag to pieces before the LORD at Gilgal.
34 Then Samuel went to Ramah. Saul went up to his home in Gibeah of Saul.
35 Until the day Samuel died, he never visited Saul again. Samuel was sad about Saul. And the LORD was sorry that He had made Saul king over Israel.

title: 1 Samuel 16
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 The LORD said to Samuel, "How long will you feel sad about Saul?" The LORD went on, "I have turned away from him as king over Israel." "Fill your horn with oil and go. I am sending you to Jesse of Bethlehem." "I have chosen a king for Myself from his sons."
2 "How can I go?" Samuel asked. "Saul will hear about it and kill me!" The LORD answered, "Take a young cow with you." "Say, 'I have come to make a sacrifice to the LORD.'"
3 "Then invite Jesse to the sacrifice. I will show you what to do." "You will anoint the one I point out."
4 So Samuel did what the LORD had said. He went to Bethlehem. The town leaders met him and shook with fear. They asked, "Do you come in peace?"

5 "In peace," he replied. "I have come to make a sacrifice to the LORD." "Make yourselves ready for God, and come with me to the sacrifice." Then he made Jesse and his sons ready, and invited them to the sacrifice.
6 When they arrived, Samuel saw Eliab. He said, "Surely the LORD's chosen one stands here."

7 But the LORD said to Samuel, "Do not look at how he looks or how tall he is." "I have turned away from him. The LORD does not see as people see." **The LORD does not look at the things people look at. People look at the outward appearance, but the LORD looks at the heart.**
8 Then Jesse called Abinadab and brought him to Samuel. Samuel said, "The LORD has not chosen this one either."
9 Next Jesse brought Shammah forward. But Samuel said, "The LORD has not chosen this one either."
10 So Jesse made seven of his sons pass before Samuel. But Samuel told him, "The LORD has not chosen any of these."

11 Samuel asked him, "Are these all the sons you have?" "There is still the youngest," Jesse replied. "But he is taking care of the sheep." "Send for him," Samuel said. "We will not sit down to eat until he arrives."
12 So Jesse sent for his youngest son and brought him in. He had a healthy red face, beautiful eyes, and a fine look. The LORD said, "Get up and anoint him. He is the one."
13 So Samuel took the horn of oil. He anointed him in front of his brothers. The Spirit of the LORD came on David with power from that day on. Then Samuel set out and went to Ramah.
14 The Spirit of the LORD left Saul. An evil spirit from the LORD began to trouble him.
15 Saul's servants said to him, "Surely an evil spirit from God is troubling you."
16 "Let our lord tell us to look for someone who plays the harp well." "When the evil spirit from God comes on you, he will play. Then you will feel better."
17 Saul said to his servants, "Find me someone who plays well. Bring him to me."

18 One of the servants answered, "I have seen a son of Jesse of Bethlehem." "He knows how to play the harp. He is a brave fighter and a warrior." "He speaks well and looks fine. And the LORD is with him."
19 So Saul sent messengers to Jesse. He said, "Send me your son David, who is with the sheep."
20 Jesse took a donkey loaded with bread, a skin of wine, and one young goat. He sent them to Saul with his son David.
21 David came to Saul and began to serve him. Saul liked him very much. David became the man who carried his armor.
22 Then Saul sent word to Jesse: "Let David stay in my service. I am happy with him."
23 Whenever the spirit from God came on Saul, David would pick up his harp and play. Then Saul would feel better, and the evil spirit would leave him.

title: 1 Samuel 17
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 The Philistines gathered their armies for war at Socoh in Judah. They camped between Socoh and Azekah, in Ephes-dammim.
2 Saul and the men of Israel gathered and camped in the Valley of Elah. They lined up to fight the Philistines.
3 The Philistines stood on one hill. The Israelites stood on another hill. The valley lay between them.
4 Then a champion came out from the Philistine camp. His name was Goliath. He was from Gath. He was **six cubits and a span** tall (about 3 meters).
5 He wore a bronze helmet on his head. He wore a bronze **coat of armor that weighed 5,000 shekels** (about 57 kg).
6 He had bronze armor on his legs. He carried a bronze javelin on his shoulders.
7 His spear pole was as thick as a weaver's beam. Its iron point weighed 600 shekels. A shield bearer walked in front of him.
8 Goliath stood and shouted to the army of Israel. "Why do you come out to fight? I am a Philistine. You are servants of Saul. Choose one man to come down and fight me.
9 If he can fight me and kill me, we will be your servants. But if I beat him and kill him, you will be our servants and work for us."
10 Then the Philistine said, "Today I challenge the army of Israel! Send me a man to fight!"
11 When Saul and all the Israelites heard him, they were shocked and very afraid.
12 Now David was the son of a man named Jesse. Jesse was an Ephrathite from Bethlehem in Judah. He had eight sons. In the days of Saul, Jesse was old.
13 Jesse's three oldest sons had gone with Saul to the war. The firstborn was Eliab. The second was Abinadab. The third was Shammah.
14 David was the youngest. The three oldest sons followed Saul.
15 But David went back and forth from Saul to care for his father's sheep in Bethlehem.
16 For forty days the Philistine came out every morning and evening to take his stand.
17 One day Jesse said to his son David, "Take this basket of roasted grain and these ten loaves of bread. Hurry to your brothers' camp.
18 Take these ten pieces of cheese to their commander. Check on your brothers. Bring back word from them.
19 They are with Saul and all the men of Israel in the Valley of Elah, fighting the Philistines."
20 So David got up early in the morning. He left the sheep with a keeper. He loaded his things and set out, as Jesse had told him. He reached the camp as the army marched out, shouting the war cry.
21 Israel and the Philistines lined up against each other.
22 David left his supplies with the supply officer. He ran to the battle line. He asked his brothers how they were.
23 While he was talking with them, Goliath the Philistine from Gath came out again. He shouted his usual words. David heard them.
24 When all the men of Israel saw Goliath, they ran from him in great fear.

25 The men of Israel had been saying, "Do you see this man who keeps coming out to insult Israel? The king will give great riches to the man who kills him. He will also give him his daughter to marry. He will free his father's family from taxes in Israel."

26 David asked the men beside him, "What will be done for the man who kills this Philistine and removes this shame from Israel? Who is this pagan Philistine, that he insults the armies of the living God?"
27 The people told him about the offer. They said, "That is what will be done for the man who kills him."

28 David's oldest brother Eliab heard him talking to the men. He became angry with David. "Why did you come down here?" he asked. "With whom did you leave those few sheep in the wilderness? I know your pride and your wicked heart. You came down to watch the battle!"
29 "What have I done now?" said David. "Was it only a question?"
30 Then he turned to another man and asked the same thing. The people answered him the same way.
31 David's words were heard and told to Saul. Saul called for him.
32 David said to Saul, "Let no man lose heart because of this Philistine. Your servant will go and fight him!"
33 But Saul replied, "You cannot go out and fight this Philistine. You are only a boy. He has been a fighter since his youth."
34 David replied, "Your servant has been caring for his father's sheep. When a lion or a bear came and took a lamb from the flock,
35 I went after it, hit it, and saved the lamb from its mouth. If it turned on me, I grabbed its fur, struck it down, and killed it.
36 Your servant has killed lions and bears. This pagan Philistine will be like one of them. He has insulted the armies of the living God."
37 David added, "The LORD saved me from the claws of the lion and the bear. He will save me from the hand of this Philistine." Saul said, "Go, and may the LORD be with you."
38 Then Saul dressed David in his own tunic. He put a bronze helmet on his head and dressed him in armor.
39 David tied his sword over the tunic and tried to walk. But he was not used to them. "I cannot walk in these," David said to Saul. "I am not used to them." So David took them off.
40 David took his staff in his hand. He chose **five smooth stones from the brook** and put them in his shepherd's bag. With his sling in hand, he walked toward the Philistine.
41 The Philistine came closer and closer to David, with his shield bearer in front of him.
42 When the Philistine looked at David, he hated him because David was only a boy with red cheeks and a handsome face.
43 "Am I a dog," he said to David, "that you come at me with sticks?" And the Philistine cursed David by his gods.
44 "Come here," he called to David, "and I will give your flesh to the birds of the air and the wild animals!"

45 But David said to the Philistine, "**You come against me with sword and spear and javelin, but I come against you in the name of the LORD Almighty**, the God of the armies of Israel, whom you have insulted.

46 Today the LORD will give you into my hand. I will strike you down and cut off your head. I will give the dead bodies of the Philistines to the birds of the air and the wild animals. Then the whole world will know that there is a God in Israel.
47 And all who are gathered here will know that the LORD does not save with sword or spear. **The battle is the LORD's**, and He will give all of you into our hands."
48 As the Philistine moved forward to attack him, David ran quickly toward the battle line to meet him.
49 David reached into his bag and took out a stone. He slung it and hit the Philistine on the forehead. The stone sank into his forehead. He fell facedown on the ground.
50 So David beat the Philistine with a sling and a stone. Without a sword in his hand, he struck down the Philistine and killed him.

51 David ran and stood over him. He grabbed the Philistine's sword, pulled it from its case, and killed him. Then he cut off his head with the sword. When the Philistines saw that their hero was dead, they turned and ran.
52 Then the men of Israel and Judah rose with a shout and chased the Philistines to the gate of Gath and to the gates of Ekron. Dead Philistines lay along the Shaaraim road to Gath and Ekron.
53 When the Israelites returned from chasing the Philistines, they took things from their camps.
54 David took the head of the Philistine and brought it to Jerusalem. He put Goliath's weapons in his own tent.

55 When Saul had watched David go out to meet the Philistine, he said to Abner, the commander of the army, "Abner, whose son is this young man?" Abner replied, "As surely as you live, O king, I do not know."
56 "Find out whose son this young man is!" said the king.
57 So when David returned from killing the Philistine, he was still holding his head in his hand. Abner took him and brought him before Saul.
58 "Whose son are you, young man?" asked Saul. "I am the son of your servant Jesse of Bethlehem," David replied.

title: 1 Samuel 18
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 After David finished speaking with Saul, the heart of Jonathan was joined to the heart of David. Jonathan **loved him as himself**.
2 From that day, Saul kept David with him. He did not let David return to his father's house.
3 Then Jonathan **made a covenant** with David, because he loved him as himself.
4 Jonathan took off the robe he was wearing and gave it to David. He also gave him his tunic, his sword, his bow, and his belt.
5 So David went out and did well in everything Saul sent him to do. Saul put him in charge of the soldiers. This pleased all the people, and Saul's officers too.

6 As the soldiers were returning home after David killed the Philistine, the women came out from all the cities of Israel. They came to meet King Saul with singing and dancing, with joyful songs and tambourines.
7 As the women danced, they sang out: **"Saul has killed his thousands, and David his tens of thousands."**
8 Saul was very angry and hated this song. "They have given the praise of tens of thousands to David," he said, "but only thousands to me. What more can he have but the kingdom?"
9 From that day on, Saul watched David with jealousy.
10 The next day a troubling spirit from God came on Saul. He spoke wildly inside the house while David played the harp as usual. Saul was holding a spear,
11 and he threw it. He thought, "I will pin David to the wall." But David got away from him twice.
12 So Saul was afraid of David, because the LORD was with David but had left Saul.
13 Therefore Saul sent David away. He gave him command of a thousand men. David led the soldiers out to battle and back.
14 He kept doing well in all his ways, because the LORD was with him.
15 When Saul saw that David was very successful, he was afraid of him.
16 But all Israel and Judah loved David, because he led them out to battle and back.

17 Then Saul said to David, "Here is my older daughter Merab. I will give her to you in marriage. Only be brave for me and fight the LORD's battles." But Saul was thinking, "I will not raise my hand against him. Let the Philistines kill him."
18 David said to Saul, "Who am I? What is my family or my father's clan in Israel, that I should become the king's son-in-law?"
19 So when the time came to give Saul's daughter Merab to David, she was given in marriage to Adriel of Meholah.
20 Now Saul's daughter Michal loved David. When this was told to Saul, it pleased him.
21 "I will give her to David," Saul thought. "She will be a trap to him, and the Philistines will kill him." So Saul said to David, "For a second time, you can be my son-in-law."
22 Then Saul ordered his servants, "Speak to David quietly. Say to him, 'Look, the king is pleased with you, and all his servants love you. Now become his son-in-law.'"
23 But when Saul's servants told these words to David, he replied, "Do you think it is a small thing to be the king's son-in-law? I am a poor man and not important."
24 The servants told Saul what David had said.
25 Saul replied, "Say to David, 'The king wants no other bride price but a hundred Philistine foreskins as revenge on his enemies.'" But Saul planned for the Philistines to kill David.
26 When the servants told these words to David, he was pleased to become the king's son-in-law. Before the wedding day arrived,

27 David and his men went out and killed two hundred Philistines. He brought their foreskins and gave them as full payment to become the king's son-in-law. Then Saul gave his daughter Michal to David in marriage.
28 When Saul saw that the LORD was with David, and that his daughter Michal loved David,
29 he grew even more afraid of David. So from then on Saul was David's enemy.
30 Every time the Philistine commanders came out for battle, David did better than all of Saul's officers. So his name was greatly honored.

title: 1 Samuel 19
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 Then Saul told his son Jonathan and all his servants to kill David. But Jonathan loved David very much,
2 so he warned David. He said, "My father Saul wants to kill you. Be careful in the morning. Find a secret place and hide there.
3 I will go out and stand by my father in the field where you are. I will ask him about you. If I learn anything, I will tell you."
4 Then Jonathan said good things about David to his father Saul. He said, "The king should not sin against his servant David. He has not sinned against you. His actions have helped you a lot.
5 He risked his life when he killed the Philistine. The LORD saved all Israel through him. You saw it and were glad. Why would you kill an innocent man like David for no reason?"
6 Saul listened to Jonathan and made a strong promise. He said, "As the LORD lives, David will not be killed."
7 So Jonathan called David and told him all these things. Then Jonathan brought David to Saul. David served Saul as he had before.
8 War started again. David went out and fought the Philistines. He beat them so badly that they ran away from him.
9 But Saul sat in his house with his spear in his hand. A bad spirit from the LORD came on him. David was playing the harp.
10 Saul tried to nail him to the wall with his spear. But the spear hit the wall. David got away from him. He ran away and escaped that night.

11 Then Saul sent messengers to David's house. They were to watch him and kill him in the morning. But David's wife Michal warned him. She said, "If you do not run for your life tonight, you will die tomorrow!"
12 So Michal let David down from the window. He ran away and escaped.
13 Then Michal took a family idol and laid it in the bed. She put some goat hair on its head. She covered it with clothes.
14 Saul sent the messengers to take David. But Michal said, "He is sick."
15 But Saul sent the messengers back to see David. He told them, "Bring him up to me in his bed so I can kill him."
16 The messengers came in. They saw the idol in the bed with goat hair on its head.
17 Saul said to Michal, "Why did you trick me like this? You sent my enemy away, and he has escaped!" Michal said, "He said to me, 'Help me get away, or I will kill you!'"
18 So David ran away and escaped. He went to Samuel at Ramah. He told him everything Saul had done to him. Then he and Samuel went to Naioth and stayed there.
19 Saul was told, "David is at Naioth in Ramah."

20 He sent messengers to capture him. They saw the group of prophets speaking as prophets, with Samuel leading them. The Spirit of God came on Saul's messengers, and they also began to speak as prophets.
21 Saul was told about this. He sent more messengers, but they began to speak as prophets too. So Saul sent messengers a third time, and they also began to speak as prophets.
22 Finally, Saul himself went to Ramah. He came to the deep water hole at Secu. He asked, "Where are Samuel and David?" Someone said, "At Naioth in Ramah."
23 So Saul went to Naioth in Ramah. But the Spirit of God came on Saul too. He walked along speaking as a prophet until he came to Naioth in Ramah.
24 Then Saul took off his clothes. He also spoke as a prophet before Samuel. He fell down with no clothes on all that day and night. That is why people say, "Is Saul also one of the prophets?"

title: 1 Samuel 20
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 Then David ran away from Naioth in Ramah. He came to Jonathan. He asked, "What have I done? What is my sin? How have I sinned against your father? Why does he want to kill me?"
2 "No, never!" Jonathan replied. "You will not die. My father does nothing, big or small, without telling me. So why would he hide this from me? It is not true!"
3 But David said again, "Your father knows you like me. He has said, 'Jonathan must not know, or he will be sad.' As the LORD lives, and as you live, I am one step from death."
4 Then Jonathan said to David, "Whatever you want, I will do for you."
5 So David told him, "Look, tomorrow is the New Moon. I should eat with the king. But let me go and hide in the field until the third evening."
6 "If your father notices I am gone, tell him this. 'David asked me to let him hurry to Bethlehem, his hometown. His family has a yearly sacrifice there.'"
7 "If he says, 'Good,' then I am safe. But if he gets very angry, you will know he has evil plans."
8 "So be kind and true to me. You made a covenant with me before the LORD. If I have done wrong, kill me yourself. Why bring me to your father?"
9 "Never!" Jonathan replied. "If I knew my father planned evil against you, would I not tell you?"
10 Then David asked Jonathan, "Who will tell me if your father answers you harshly?"
11 "Come," he replied. "Let us go out to the field." So the two of them went out into the field.
12 Jonathan said, "By the LORD, the God of Israel, I will test my father. I will do this by this time tomorrow or the next day."

13 "If he favors you, I will send for you and tell you. But if my father plans to harm you, may the LORD punish me severely. I will tell you and send you away safely. May the LORD be with you, as He was with my father."
14 "As long as I live, show me the LORD's faithful love, so I will not die."
15 "Never cut off your faithful love from my family. Do not stop, even when the LORD cuts off all David's enemies from the earth."
16 So Jonathan made a covenant with the house of David. He said, "May the LORD hold David's enemies accountable."
17 Jonathan made David promise again, because of his love for him. Jonathan loved David as he loved himself.
18 Then Jonathan said to David, "Tomorrow is the New Moon. They will notice that your seat is empty."
19 "After three days, hurry down to the place you hid before. Wait beside the stone called Ezel."
20 "I will shoot three arrows beside it, as if I am aiming at a target."
21 "Then I will send a boy. I will say, 'Go, find the arrows!' If I say, 'Look, the arrows are on this side of you; bring them,' then come. As the LORD lives, you are safe."
22 "But if I say to the boy, 'Look, the arrows are beyond you,' then you must go. The LORD has sent you away."
23 "About what you and I have said — the LORD is a witness between us forever."
24 So David hid in the field. When the New Moon came, the king sat down to eat.
25 He sat in his usual place by the wall. Jonathan sat across from him. Abner sat beside him. But David's place was empty.
26 Saul said nothing that day. He thought, "Something has happened to David. He is not pure for worship today."
27 But on the second day, David's place was still empty. Saul asked his son Jonathan, "Why has the son of Jesse not come to the meal — yesterday or today?"
28 Jonathan answered, "David asked me to let him go to Bethlehem."

29 "He said, 'Please let me go. Our family is holding a sacrifice in the city. My brother told me to be there. If you favor me, let me go and see my brothers.' That is why he did not come to the king's table."
30 Then Saul became very angry with Jonathan. He said, "You son of a wicked and stubborn woman! I know you have chosen the son of Jesse. You bring shame on yourself and on your mother."
31 "As long as the son of Jesse lives on this earth, your kingdom will not stand. Send for him and bring him to me. He must die!"
32 "Why must he die?" Jonathan replied. "What has he done?"
33 Then Saul threw his spear at Jonathan to kill him. So Jonathan knew his father was determined to kill David.
34 Jonathan got up from the table in great anger. He ate no food on the second day of the month. He was sad because of his father's shameful actions against David.
35 In the morning, Jonathan went out to the field. He went to meet David. A small boy was with him.
36 He said to the boy, "Run and find the arrows I shoot." As the boy ran, Jonathan shot an arrow beyond him.
37 When the boy reached the place where the arrow had fallen, Jonathan called to him, "Is the arrow not beyond you?"
38 Then Jonathan cried out, "Hurry! Be quick! Do not wait!" So the boy picked up the arrow and returned to his master.
39 But the boy knew nothing. Only Jonathan and David knew the plan.
40 Then Jonathan gave his weapons to the boy. He said, "Go, take them back to the city."

41 When the boy had gone, David got up from the south side of the stone. He bowed down with his face to the ground three times. Then he and Jonathan kissed each other and wept together. But David wept more.

42 Jonathan said to David, "Go in peace. We have both made a promise in the LORD's name. We said, 'The LORD is a witness between you and me. He is a witness between your children and mine forever.'" Then David got up and left. Jonathan went back into the city.

title: 1 Samuel 21
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Samuel
paragraphs: 1 Then David came to Nob, to Ahimelech the priest. Ahimelech met David and shook with fear. He asked, "Why are you alone? Why is no one with you?"
2 "The king has given me a special job," David replied. "He told me to keep it secret. I have told my young men to meet me at a certain place."
3 "What food do you have? Give me five loaves of bread, or anything you can find."
4 The priest replied, "I have no normal bread here. But I have some holy bread. Your young men must have stayed away from women."
